items.title: Constraint-first reachability check
items.description: When an operation limit is small, determine reachability by directly testing the allowed moves instead of guessing a pattern.
items.content: If the problem restricts the number of operations (like “at most one swap”) and the state space is tiny, enumerate all legal actions and verify whether any result matches the goal. This prevents logically incorrect assumptions and eliminates edge-case mistakes.
items.reasoning: Successful attempts consistently verified the operation constraint (“at most once swap”) by explicitly checking whether the target arrangement is reachable, either by enumerating all possible swaps (safe for tiny state spaces) or by using a necessary-and-sufficient condition derived from mismatch positions and then still validating with the swap result. Unsuccessful/partially correct attempts failed by oversimplifying the logic (e.g., answering YES only for one specific permutation) or by making an incorrect unconditional claim (e.g., returning YES for all permutations), without tying the response to the actual reachability under the single-swap constraint.

items.title: Use invariant-like mismatch reasoning (then verify)
items.description: Convert informal conditions into a formal mismatch condition (e.g., differing positions), and validate any derived criterion by checking the resulting transformation.
items.content: For permutations, track which positions differ between the current state and the target; a single swap only can fix characters in specific ways. After deriving a condition (such as requiring exactly two mismatched indices), still apply the swap and confirm the final string matches the target to guard against overlooked cases.

items.title: Avoid oversimplified criteria that ignore operation constraints
items.description: Don’t answer based solely on partial properties (like already-equal checks) when the task depends on permitted transformations.
items.content: Common failure mode is reducing the decision to a single equality test or an unconditional claim, which ignores the “can be fixed with at most one action” requirement. Ensure the output is justified by whether the allowed operation(s) can transform the input into the target.

items.title: Greedy with “leftmost uncovered” invariant
items.description: Maintain an invariant that all positions up to a certain index are already guaranteed removed, and whenever you find the first uncovered black you must act immediately.
items.content: Scan left-to-right and track the furthest index that has been covered by prior operations. When you meet a black cell beyond that boundary, start an operation that covers it and then advance the boundary by k; this prevents double-counting and ensures optimality because you never postpone covering the leftmost remaining requirement.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts share a simple left-to-right greedy with a “cleared/uncovered” pointer: each time you encounter a black cell that hasn’t been covered yet, you start an operation as far left as possible (at that cell) so it becomes white and you can skip ahead by k. Failures typically come from confusing how to handle indices after an operation (e.g., jumping incorrectly, not truly ensuring previously covered cells are skipped, or assuming segment-based formulas without accounting for interactions across gaps). Another failure mode is overcomplicating with DP and then not implementing the correct transitions/invariants, or using transformations/extra logic (“start at max(0, n-k)”) that can be valid only with a more careful proof and coverage check.

items.title: Skip by coverage rather than by local pattern
items.description: After applying an operation, advance the scan index based on the operation’s guaranteed effect (the covered interval), not on the current characters.
items.content: Instead of re-checking every cell in the affected window, jump past the entire region that the operation makes irrelevant. This reduces logic errors where remaining blacks inside the window might be missed or where whites inside the window incorrectly influence decisions.

items.description: Formulas like “count blocks and take a ceiling” only work when you have proven operations cannot be shared across separated parts in a way that changes the count.
items.content: If you derive an answer from maximal contiguous segments, explicitly justify whether operations can “bridge” gaps (or whether each segment must be handled independently). When such independence is unclear, prefer a coverage/invariant greedy or a correctly defined DP.

items.description: When using DP, ensure the state represents exactly the information needed to make optimal next choices; otherwise the implementation may silently be wrong.
items.content: Several attempts attempted DP but relied on partial or incorrect equivalences without fully encoding how future operations can cover earlier requirements. If you can’t precisely define the invariant “what part of the string is already cleared” or the set of reachable starts, default to a simpler greedy with a proven coverage pointer.

items.title: Parity-Only Palindrome Feasibility
items.description: When rearrangement is allowed, reduce palindrome requirements to constraints on how many character counts are odd.
items.content: For any multiset of letters, it can be permuted into a palindrome iff the number of odd-frequency letters is 0 (even length) or 1 (odd length). This lets you ignore character identities and work only with the odd-count count, turning the problem into a parity/state-transition check.
items.reasoning: Successful attempts consistently modeled the problem at the level of parity of character frequencies rather than exact counts, because rearranging allows any multiset arrangement. The key invariant is: deleting one character toggles the parity of exactly one letter’s frequency, so the number of odd-frequency letters changes by ±1 per deletion, and its parity relative to the number of deletions is fixed. Incorrect attempts often used incomplete or overconfident “bounds” (e.g., claiming parity conditions alone guarantee feasibility, or using formulas that mishandled the required number of odd counts for even/odd remaining length, especially by assuming minimum “odd adjustments” equals a simple absolute difference without careful parity of the surplus deletions).

items.title: Deletion as a Parity-Toggle Transition
items.description: Model each deletion as toggling exactly one letter’s frequency parity, hence changing the odd-count count by ±1.
items.content: Since removing one occurrence flips the parity of that letter’s frequency, the “number of odd-frequency letters” changes by exactly 1 in magnitude per deletion. Therefore, reachability to a target odd-count depends on (a) whether the target is within distance k and (b) parity compatibility of the remaining “slack” deletions.

items.title: Distance + Same-Parity Check for Reachability
items.description: To decide if a state can be reached in exactly k parity-flip steps, require both |delta| ≤ k and (k − |delta|) even.
items.content: When each step changes a quantity by ±1, then after k steps the quantity must differ from the start by an integer with the same parity as k; equivalently (k − required_change) must be even. This pattern generalizes to many “±1 per move” reachability problems on integer-valued invariants.

items.title: Separate Necessity From Sufficiency Carefully
items.description: Do not assume parity conditions alone guarantee feasibility unless the state-transition model proves sufficiency.
items.content: Some incorrect solutions effectively treated a derived equation or parity constraint as automatically satisfiable, but missed that additional constraints (like correct target odd-count for even/odd remaining length, or parity of slack deletions) can make the transition impossible. Always verify both minimal-step feasibility and parity compatibility with the exact “in k moves” requirement.

items.title: Factor k and reduce to v_p(Product) exponents
items.description: Rewrite the divisibility goal in terms of prime-factor exponents of k and the product’s prime valuations.
items.content: For k up to a small constant, factor it into primes and require that the product’s total v_p is at least the corresponding exponent from k. This turns a global “product divisible by k” condition into independent exponent-threshold constraints that are far easier to reason about and compute.
items.reasoning: Across attempts, the key pattern is the difference between correct solutions that properly model the required divisibility condition and unsuccessful/partially-correct ones that oversimplify it. The successful approach reduces the task to prime-factor requirements of k and then computes the minimum increments by considering how many extra prime factors each incremented element can contribute, using a small DP/knapsack because the needed exponent amounts are tiny for k≤5. Failures mainly come from assuming “modulo k” or residue information (Attempt 2) is sufficient, or from treating factor acquisition as if it were only about “next multiple” costs or only one prime’s exponent count without rigorously addressing the exact exponent shortfall and how increments can simultaneously affect multiple factors (Attempts 1,2,3,4,6 partially-correct variants). Another recurrent failure mode is brittle casework on k where cost formulas are derived from simplistic periodicity; these formulas can be wrong because achieving an exponent gain isn’t determined solely by a single modular step—what matters is when v_p(a_i) crosses thresholds.

items.title: Model increments as “how many extra factors thresholds each element can reach”
items.description: For each element, compute the minimum cost to increase its contribution by reaching specific v_p thresholds, not just by meeting a residue or divisibility once.
items.content: An increment changes v_p(a_i) only when crossing certain additive boundaries; therefore derive costs for gaining 0/1/2 (or the needed amount) extra prime factors by explicitly evaluating a small bounded range of increments. Then combine these per-element “factor-gain options” with a small selection/DP to cover the remaining deficit.

items.title: Use small-state DP/knapsack when required deficit is tiny
items.description: When k is small, the needed prime-exponent deficit is tiny, enabling DP over deficit states rather than exponential search.
items.content: Instead of greedy or broad enumeration, use DP where the state tracks how much of the required exponent deficit has been satisfied. This is efficient because the number of exponent units needed (e.g., up to 2 for k=4) is small, making transitions tractable.

items.title: Validate risky simplifications: “modulo k” is not always enough
items.description: Do not assume that tracking product residues mod k captures all exponent requirements when k has repeated prime factors.
items.content: Attempts that relied on product mod k or residue-period arguments can fail because higher prime powers (e.g., needing v_p≥2) are not determined by a single modulo class when factors are non-coprime or composite-power dependent. Always verify that the representation you track is equivalent to the original exponent condition.

items.title: Reformulate as a state game using the win-check timing
items.description: When outcomes depend on “after a specific player’s move,” model that timing explicitly instead of reasoning only about distances to targets.
items.content: Turn the narrative into a game-state model that captures whose turn it is and when the win condition is evaluated (e.g., only after one player’s move). This prevents off-by-one reasoning where you incorrectly assume the win can occur after the opponent’s move or at the wrong ply.
items.reasoning: The successful solutions converge on a simple modular characterization, while most incorrect/partial attempts made unjustified assumptions about “reachability distance” (e.g., treating the problem as if existence of a small path to a multiple directly determines the winner) or used the wrong time/turn condition (win is checked only after the first player’s move, and the game ends after a fixed number of total moves). items.title: State-Invariant DP for Constrained Subarrays
items.description: Maintain an invariant DP value that represents the optimal solution among subarrays ending at the current index.
items.content: When a problem asks for a maximum over subarrays with adjacency constraints, use a DP state like “best sum of a valid subarray ending at i.” Then update it by either extending from i−1 (if allowed) or restarting at i (if not). This prevents accumulating illegal candidates and works cleanly even with negative numbers.
items.reasoning: Successful attempts consistently used a “best subarray ending at i” dynamic update: when a local constraint between adjacent elements is satisfied, the optimal valid subarray can extend; otherwise it must restart at the current element. Unsuccessful attempts typically deviated by only tracking segment length/sum without correctly handling cases where restarting is mandatory, or by using an incorrect initialization/logic for when extension is allowed (leading to wrong values when sequences include negatives). The key distinguishing factor is maintaining the correct invariant: the running value represents the maximum sum among all valid subarrays that end at the current position.

items.title: Local Constraint → Binary Transition (Extend vs Restart)
items.description: Convert the global alternating condition into a local check that determines whether you can extend the current DP state.
items.content: Alternating/compatibility constraints on adjacent elements can be handled with a simple local predicate between a[i−1] and a[i]. If the predicate holds, the transition should consider “extend” (DP+value) versus “start fresh” (value alone); if it fails, restarting is mandatory. This yields O(n) per test case and avoids incorrect mixing across invalid boundaries.

items.title: Validate Initialization and Negative-Safe Starts
items.description: Choose initialization that guarantees correctness when all values could be negative.
items.content: For maximum subarray problems, initialize the running best and current state directly from the first element (or with a sufficiently negative sentinel) to ensure the algorithm always selects a non-empty subarray. Common failures come from starting with neutral values (e.g., zero) or from incorrect “extend vs start” conditions that only work for non-negative data.

items.title: Use a Direct Formula: cur = max(value, cur + value) When Allowed
items.description: When extension is allowed, the optimal choice is the maximum of starting anew or extending the current best state.
items.content: If adjacency constraints permit extension, compute the new DP state with a standard max between “start at i” and “extend previous best.” This pattern is a reliable way to avoid subtle errors in ad-hoc segment tracking (like maintaining length without the correct restart/value tradeoff). A final pass updating the global answer from the DP state completes the approach.

items.title: Constraint-First: Reduce to an Exact Equality in a Computable Domain
items.description: Turn the original exponential condition into an explicit algebraic equation over exponents before attempting any counting.
items.content: Start by rewriting the condition (often by taking logs via base-2 uniqueness) until it becomes a deterministic equation such as a linear/exponential relation on the input integers. Avoid substituting partial intuitions (“group by odd part”, “it must be consecutive”) until the transformation is proven to be necessary and sufficient.
items.reasoning: The successful attempt derives the condition into a precise algebraic relation on the original exponents and then counts solutions by enumerating the small set of feasible structural relationships, while keeping the counting logic separate from the number-theory test. The unsuccessful attempts mostly fail by using intuitively plausible but incorrect characterizations (e.g., “same odd part implies validity” or “only pairs of the form (x,2x)”), or by performing risky transformations without validating them against the original equation. Some partially correct attempts reduce the equation in right directions but then jump to an unjustified final form; others choose a candidate key (like a rational normalization) that doesn’t actually preserve equality for the given exponential structure. Overall: success came from careful equation transformation to an exact necessary-and-sufficient condition, then a counting strategy that only aggregates over truly valid pairs; failure came from overgeneralizing after an incomplete or incorrect equivalence.

items.title: Validate Every “Known Trick” With a Direct Equivalence Check
items.description: Any proposed characterization of valid pairs must be verified against the original equality, not just against examples.
items.content: Several failed attempts assumed a commonly seen pattern (like “same odd part” or “only (x,2x) cases”) without proving it, leading to systematic wrong results. When you derive a rule, immediately test it by substituting back into the original equation to confirm no extra solutions (false positives) or missing solutions (false negatives).

items.description: Compute all structurally valid value-pairs first, then multiply by frequencies to count indices.

items.title: Reformulate the Process as Layered Maxima After Row Sorting
items.description: Turn repeated “remove row maximums then add the global maximum among removed” into a deterministic round-by-round computation using sorted rows.
items.content: Sort each row in descending order and treat the removals as advancing one “pointer” per row per round. At round k, the only candidates removed in that round correspond to the k-th elements (by sorted order) of rows that still have elements, and the score increment is the maximum among those candidates. This makes the global coupling (“max across rows”) local per round.
items.reasoning: All “correct” attempts converge on the same core abstraction: the process can be viewed in rounds/layers after sorting each row in descending order, where at each layer the value added to the score is the maximum among the currently available elements from each row at that layer index (or equivalently, the max among current row heads). The partially correct attempts demonstrate common failure modes: (1) trying to reason about the global order of removals using an oversimplified reduction (“sum of all elements sorted descending” / “layer maxima equals global k-th maximum”) without a rigorous coupling between rounds and positions, leading to confusion about multiplicities and which elements are actually available when; and (2) implementing a simulation that doesn’t preserve the invariant correctly (e.g., advancing pointers for all occurrences of a chosen maximum at the head, which can skip elements from other rows’ current heads and break the round-by-round structure). The unsuccessful partially correct heap-based approach conceptually matches the correct model (each round takes the maximum of row maxima), but the “partially correct” labeling suggests a discrepancy likely due to state/invariant mismatch or boundary handling; in contrast, the fully correct solutions use either direct “layered max” computation or an equivalent max-heap removal while preserving the per-row “current head” invariant.

items.title: Preserve a Clear Invariant: “Current Head per Row”
items.description: When simulating, ensure your state always represents exactly what each row exposes as its current maximum.
items.content: Whether you use pointers over sorted rows or a heap, keep the invariant that each row contributes its current largest remaining element (its head). Then the algorithm’s decision each step (“choose the overall maximum among row heads”) is correct by construction, and you avoid skipping or double-removing elements inadvertently. Any update rule that moves multiple elements in a row per step without matching the real “one removal per non-empty row” rule will likely break the invariant.

items.title: Subset DP or Enumeration When Constraints Are Small
items.description: If the input size is small enough, prefer DP/bitmask enumeration to avoid brittle casework and sign errors.
items.content: When n is small, enumerating all non-empty subsets (or meet-in-the-middle) directly evaluates candidates and sidesteps tricky correctness conditions. This is especially valuable for “maximize product with sign constraints” problems where greedy reasoning can fail on edge cases like zeros or all-negative arrays.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts share a reliable core idea: model the choice as a subset problem where the key global constraint is the sign (especially parity of the number of negative factors), then optimize accordingly. The simplest brute-force (bitmask subset enumeration) succeeds because the input size is tiny and it avoids subtle reasoning errors about sign/zero corner cases. The partially/unsuccessful attempts fail mainly due to (1) brittle sign DP transitions that mix “max positive” with “min negative” semantics inconsistently, (2) incorrect or unsafe handling of the non-empty requirement and empty-product initialization, and/or (3) “greedy/heuristic” logic that seems standard but breaks in corner cases involving zeros and when the best product is non-positive (e.g., deciding whether to keep or drop negatives when zeros exist). items.title: Sort and Decompose by Extreme with a Rolling Weighted Aggregate
items.description: When group power depends on both min and max, sort and decompose contributions by fixing one extreme, then compute the other extreme’s aggregated effect via a single rolling weighted sum.
items.content: After sorting, every subset has a well-defined position of its maximum (or minimum), which lets you count all subsets with that extreme in one sweep. The key is to translate the min-dependent term into a weighted sum over earlier elements, then maintain that weight-sum with a recurrence that updates in O(1) per step.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts converge on a consistent “sort + partition by max (or min) in sorted order” framework and then compute the needed weighted sum using a rolling recurrence (typically updating a single aggregate like a weighted minimum-sum or a running “A_i” term) without double counting. Failures largely come from (1) trying to use an incorrect or mismatched weighting factor (e.g., confusing uniform subset counts with the required exponential distance weights), (2) mishandling duplicates by processing indices but not equal-value groups, or (3) deriving a formula but not verifying that the derived aggregate matches the exact definition of min/max for every subset, leading to subtle off-by-one or missing “empty subset” cases.

items.title: Invariant-First Simulation for Repeat-Stopping Processes
items.description: When the game ends at the first repeat of a state (e.g., ball holder), track visited states directly to guarantee correctness.
items.content: Reformulate the process as a deterministic state transition (current holder → next holder) and maintain a visited set/array. Terminate immediately upon encountering a previously visited holder, since the problem’s stopping condition is exactly that repeat.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts mostly used direct simulation with a “visited positions” invariant, correctly matching the rule that the stopping condition is a repeat (second time someone receives). They avoided relying on an unverified number-theory characterization for the winner set. In contrast, several partially-correct attempts jumped to a gcd/arithmetic-progression characterization without fully validating it against the actual “increasing step size by turn” dynamics (triangular-number/accumulated movement), leading to incorrect winner sets for some cases. The key distinction is whether the method maintains a correct invariant tied to the game’s stopping condition (repeat of holders) rather than an overgeneralized closed form.

items.title: Validate Any Closed-Form Claim Against the Actual Transition Rule
items.description: If you derive winners/losers via gcd or group structure, explicitly verify the derivation against the true transition (including changing step sizes).
items.content: It’s easy to guess a gcd-based orbit for problems with constant step size, but here the step depends on the turn, so the closed form must incorporate accumulated movement (often leading to triangular-number effects modulo n). Before trusting a formula, test it by reasoning from the exact recurrence or running a small verification simulation.

items.description: Maintain the set of receivers/holders rather than indirectly reasoning about counts, so you can output losers in sorted order reliably.
items.content: Instead of inferring which participants are winners, record which indices are ever reached. This directly yields the losers as the complement and naturally supports returning the answer in ascending order.

items.title: Seed-and-Propagate Linear Constraints
items.description: When an array transform defines each output as a local XOR relation, fix one original bit and propagate to determine the rest.
items.content: For transforms that relate adjacent elements, choosing an initial “seed” value turns the system into a deterministic recurrence, letting you compute all implied original bits efficiently. After propagation, the only remaining work is to verify the final (often wrap-around or boundary) constraint to ensure the seed is consistent.
items.reasoning: The successful approaches share a key pattern: they treat the XOR-defined derived array as linear constraints over bits, then use propagation (fixing one “seed” bit of the original) to uniquely determine the rest, and finally enforce a single wrap-around consistency condition. Several failed/partially-correct attempts stem from relying on an oversimplified global condition (e.g., total XOR equals 0) without properly accounting for how the wrap-around equation depends on the chosen seed and which derived elements are constrained. The truly correct solutions also show that different algebraic simplifications (checking last consistency vs checking prefix-vs-last vs checking total XOR) are valid only when derived constraints are handled correctly; attempts that arrived at the same condition but with incorrect reasoning or missing edge-case logic were the ones that failed.

items.title: Constraint-first reachability modeling
items.description: Reformulate the partition condition as a reachability/Dynamic Programming problem over string positions and partial sums.
items.content: When a requirement is “split into contiguous pieces whose numeric values sum to a target,” represent progress by the current position in the string and the accumulated (or remaining) sum. This yields a clear transition rule for extending the next substring and a precise success criterion when you’ve consumed the entire string.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts share a clear pattern: they formalize “can the square’s decimal string be split into contiguous substrings summing to the target” as a reachability problem over positions and partial sums, then use pruning/memoization to ensure correctness and efficiency. Failures (partially correct) come from inadequate state handling—e.g., only tracking a one-pass set of reachable sums without correctly capturing all valid partitions across positions, or prematurely concluding based on local conditions (such as hitting the target before consuming the full string) without an invariant that guarantees global correctness. Another distinguishing factor is avoiding incorrect memo keys: some versions correctly cache by (index, current sum) or (index, remaining) and ensure they represent the full relevant future state; less reliable approaches can miss valid paths or waste computation that leads to timeouts (even if conceptually right).

items.title: Model parallel effects inside the DP transition
items.description: When an action both consumes a resource and enables parallel progress, encode the combined net effect directly in the DP transition.
items.content: For problems where choosing one item generates additional work done “for free” in parallel, the DP transition should add the total reachable progress from that choice (consumption + parallel gain), not just the local action. Validate that the state meaning matches this combined effect so later decisions remain feasible.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts consistently used a DP state that correctly models the *interaction* between choosing a paid assignment (which both consumes paid “capacity” and simultaneously creates parallel free-painter progress) and later choices. They either framed the state as “remaining unpainted walls” or “already covered walls,” but crucially ensured that each transition accounted for the combined effect (paid wall + parallel free coverage) and kept the transition monotone and valid by capping at the target size. The incorrect attempts failed mainly by treating “skipping” in a way that implicitly assumes free-capacity availability without explicitly encoding or validating that resource constraint in the state. One partially correct attempt showed the same vulnerability: backward iteration/loop-range changes don’t fix an incorrect/underspecified state meaning; skipping logic must be derived from the state’s semantics.

items.title: Choose a DP state that makes invalid “skip” actions impossible
items.description: Ensure every transition (including “not taking”) is justified by explicit state semantics, so you never rely on implicit capacity assumptions.
items.content: If “skipping” an item is only allowed when some capacity/condition holds, the DP state must represent that capacity (or remaining requirement) so the transition can be conditionally applied. Otherwise, the DP may count solutions that are not physically feasible under the original constraint.

items.content: Define a progress measure (e.g., covered walls or remaining walls) that is monotone under transitions, then clamp any update to the target bound. items.title: Constraint-to-Model: make operations map to a rigorous buying rule
items.description: Before optimizing, explicitly translate each operation into how it changes which “source costs” can be assigned to each required purchase.
items.content: Several incorrect attempts relied on informal claims like “each extra item costs one shift” or “each operation lets you replace one price” without proving the mapping. The successful approaches implicitly identify a consistent rule after sorting (e.g., how many times an operation-induced replacement can be applied) and then optimize under that rule, avoiding double-counting and invalid transition logic.
items.reasoning: The “successful” attempts are those that converge on a simple structural rule after sorting—effectively modeling how many times the global operation-induced penalty can be “reused” across multiple purchases. In contrast, many incorrect attempts either (1) guessed a cost formula without a solid invariant/mapping from operations to which items can be replaced, or (2) built DP/state models that didn’t respect the true mechanics of global cyclic shifts (leading to transitions that double-count penalties or ignore rotation equivalences). Another failure pattern is checking plausibility only with intuition (e.g., “each extra purchase costs one operation”) rather than verifying the mapping by which purchased costs correspond to shifted indices.

items.title: Prefer sorting-based greedy when the objective depends only on relative costs
items.description: If an operation makes only the ordering/selection of costs matter, sort first and reduce the problem to choosing between “buy directly” vs “buy under a replaced effective cost.”
items.content: Correct solutions are characterized by converting the process into “for each cost in sorted order, take the cheaper of direct purchase vs operation-assisted purchase,” which works when the operation effect is uniform across types and the optimal plan can be arranged to match the sorted order. Wrong attempts often tried complicated DP/permutation reasoning without first checking whether sorting collapses the structure into a monotone decision problem.

items.title: Validate any “replacement” claim by checking extremal cases
items.description: When you hypothesize a formula like “replace an item with the minimum plus a fixed penalty,” test it against limiting regimes (very large/small penalty, or all equal costs) to confirm the replacement model.
items.content: Many failures show that a replacement model can be off by a constant factor (e.g., wrong number of times x should be paid) or can apply to the wrong subset. A quick verification against extremes would reveal mismatches early, before implementing an incorrect DP/greedy formula.

items.title: DP transition sanity: avoid subtracting/adding the same penalty component
items.description: When building DP with costs that include a “per-step” term, ensure transitions don’t accidentally add and remove that term inconsistently.
items.content: Some incorrect DPs attempted to compensate for added per-operation costs by subtracting t*x in one place and adding it elsewhere, which is a common source of wrong results. A reliable pattern is to define the DP state so that operation penalties are accounted for exactly once per transition, then keep transitions algebraically consistent.

items.title: Coupled-Variables Difference Modeling
items.description: When operations change multiple variables together, optimize by tracking their relative difference rather than any single variable.
items.content: Reframe the problem using an invariant or derived quantity like the difference between the two coupled values, and express how one operation changes that difference. Then maximize the target subject to the reachable range implied by that difference, which automatically accounts for the coupling.
items.reasoning: The correct attempts consistently recognized the operation as creating a bounded “relative shift” between x and num, and they derived the optimum by pushing that shift in the favorable direction every time. The incorrect attempt under-counted by assuming the only effect on x is +1, ignoring how the simultaneously changing num expands the feasible final x when you require that x can be brought back to match num within the remaining operations. In general, success came from modeling the net effect on the difference (or relative offset) between the two values, while failure came from optimizing only one variable without accounting for the coupling constraint.

items.title: Account for Feasibility of Returning to a Constraint
items.description: Maximizing a variable often still requires ensuring you can satisfy the ending condition within the operation budget.
items.content: Instead of greedily pushing toward the maximum, reason about how far you can deviate while still being able to return to (or reach) the required end relation using the remaining operations. This “deviate but stay recoverable” logic prevents underestimating the expanded feasible region caused by coupled updates.

items.title: Boundary-Change Reduction
items.description: When operations flip intervals, the minimum cost to reach a uniform string often depends only on positions where adjacent characters differ.
items.content: Instead of tracking full interval effects, identify an invariant like the “difference” between adjacent bits (or run boundaries) that must be eliminated/normalized. Then compute the minimal cost as a sum over those necessary boundary changes, because each boundary requirement corresponds to at least one operation parity change.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts (1,2,3) converge on a clean reduction: the operations affect the string only through where adjacent bits differ (“boundaries/runs”), and each required boundary “toggle” can be paid independently using the cheaper side cost. The correct solutions avoid overcomplicated parity/target DP that is easy to get wrong due to incorrect transition modeling or indexing. The incorrect/partially correct attempts show common failure modes: (a) introducing pivot or target-based formulations without correctly enforcing the global parity consistency between boundaries, (b) attempting to build a DP with parity states but using transitions that don’t properly correspond to which operations actually affect which positions, and (c) adding end/initial adjustments inconsistently (e.g., claiming the whole-string flip adds 0 vs n, or double-counting/omitting it). The main transferable insight is to model the problem in terms of invariant(s) that operations preserve/alter locally (adjacent-difference boundaries) and then compute minimal costs per boundary with validated cost mapping.

items.title: Per-Boundary Cheapest Toggle Mapping
items.description: If a model shows each required boundary toggle can be executed via choices on either side, take the minimum of those side costs per boundary.
items.content: For interval-flip problems with additive costs, once you prove boundary effects are independent (or can be composed without interference beyond parity), the total optimal cost becomes the sum of per-boundary minimum costs. Always map “what operation fixes boundary i” to an explicit cost expression and verify it matches the original operation definitions.

items.title: Earliest-difference greedy selection
items.description: To minimize lexicographically, anchor the operation at the leftmost index where you can make the resulting string strictly smaller.
items.content: Because lexicographic order is determined by the first position where two strings differ, you should choose the smallest possible starting index that yields a decrease at that position. Once that earliest “improving” position is fixed, decisions to the right should only be made if they do not introduce an earlier worsening at the first differing index.
items.reasoning: Across attempts, the successful solutions share the same core greedy structure: (1) identify the earliest index where the first differing character can be made lexicographically smaller, (2) extend the chosen contiguous substring only while each additional decremented character still helps at its own position, and (3) stop before the operation would wrap an ‘a’ to ‘z’, which would worsen the first position where they differ. Failures came from either mis-handling the “exactly once” constraint for the all-‘a’ case, choosing the wrong end boundary (e.g., stopping too early or too late), or incorrectly justifying extension when wrap-around creates an earlier worse character.

items.title: Boundary control around wrap-around
items.description: When an operation has a cyclic/wrap effect, extend only while the transformation is monotone-improving; stop before the first wrap that would make a character larger.
items.content: For digit/letter decrement operations with wrap (like ‘a’→‘z’), adding more length to the substring can suddenly turn a locally helpful change into a harmful one at the first position where wrap occurs. A reliable method is to expand the substring while the affected characters are not in the “bad” set that would wrap to a larger value, then halt just before the first such character.
items.reasoning: Across attempts, the successful solutions share the same core greedy structure: (1) identify the earliest index where the first differing character can be made lexicographically smaller, (2) extend the chosen contiguous substring only while each additional decremented character still helps at its own position, and (3) stop before the operation would wrap an ‘a’ to ‘z’, which would worsen the first position where they differ. Failures came from either mis-handling the “exactly once” constraint for the all-‘a’ case, choosing the wrong end boundary (e.g., stopping too early or too late), or incorrectly justifying extension when wrap-around creates an earlier worse character.

items.title: Special-case “all bad” carefully under “exactly once” constraint
items.description: If the string is in a state where the intended improvement is impossible, determine the optimal move using the first differing index principle rather than defaulting to “do nothing.”
items.content: When no character can be decreased without wrap (e.g., all characters are the problematic minimal letter), the operation must still be applied exactly once, even though any change is locally worse. In that case, choose the substring that pushes the first worsening as far to the right as possible, typically by altering the smallest possible prefix impact (often a single last character).
items.reasoning: Across attempts, the successful solutions share the same core greedy structure: (1) identify the earliest index where the first differing character can be made lexicographically smaller, (2) extend the chosen contiguous substring only while each additional decremented character still helps at its own position, and (3) stop before the operation would wrap an ‘a’ to ‘z’, which would worsen the first position where they differ. Failures came from either mis-handling the “exactly once” constraint for the all-‘a’ case, choosing the wrong end boundary (e.g., stopping too early or too late), or incorrectly justifying extension when wrap-around creates an earlier worse character.

items.title: Post-greedy verification of candidate endpoints
items.description: After choosing a start index greedily, explicitly verify that the chosen end boundary preserves lexicographic minimality by checking the first position where the result would diverge unfavorably.

items.title: Offline Coverage + Union Intervals for “At Least One”
items.description: Convert “server has at least one event in an interval” into time-coverage intervals, then count servers via union coverage rather than raw event counts.
items.content: When the property is existential (“at least one”), create coverage intervals induced by each event, then merge overlapping intervals per entity to avoid double counting. After unions are built, global counting can be done with sweep-line or difference arrays over query times to answer all queries efficiently.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts converge on an offline counting approach that avoids iterating over all servers per query. They recognize that each server’s “having at least one log in a window” can be transformed into coverage over time (intervals from each log, merged per server), and then a global sweep/difference structure can answer all queries at once. Failures consistently come from either (1) per-query/per-server binary searches (correct but too slow), (2) incomplete/incorrect sweep logic (missing the actual activation condition or leaving placeholders), or (3) double-counting issues when multiple logs for the same server overlap unless unions/“distinctness” are handled properly.

items.title: Choose a State Representation That Matches the Output
items.description: Select whether you track counts or only presence based on what the final result requires.
items.content: If the final answer depends only on which positions have at least one item, a set of occupied positions can suffice; but if you must know whether a move empties a position (because multiple items can accumulate and later all move away), you need a count/multiset-like state. A good check is to ask: “Does the algorithm need to know how many remain after removals, or only whether the count is zero?”
items.reasoning: Successful attempts consistently modeled the process using a state representation that preserves exactly what matters for the final answer: counts per position when multiplicity can collapse to zero after a move, or just an occupancy set when only presence/absence is relevant and the move semantics guarantee correct updates. Failures among “partially correct” approaches came from using an incorrect abstraction: treating moves as static mappings and resolving paths without accounting for the fact that the process is order-dependent and depends on evolving occupancy/counts (a later move may only apply to marbles that are at that position at that time). The correct solutions either (1) simulate in order with counts, ensuring conservation and correct removal/addition, or (2) use a set-based update but only in a way consistent with “move all marbles from X” semantics, not by assuming arbitrary reachability.

items.title: Simulate Moves in Order When Transitions Are Time-Dependent
items.description: When operations depend on the current distribution, process steps sequentially rather than composing static mappings.

Therefore, compute the next state from the current state for each step; avoid strategies that assume a later relocation applies uniformly to items that might not be present at that time.

items.title: Constraint-First State Reduction
items.description: Rewrite the multi-step operation as an equation in terms of the number of operations k, turning reachability into a feasibility test.

items.title: Offline Sweep by One Constraint
items.description: Convert a 2D dominance query into a 1D sweep plus range-maximum queries by sorting on one threshold.
items.content: Sort candidate points by the first inequality threshold in the same direction as the queries, then add points incrementally while processing queries in that order. For each query, the remaining constraints become a single range query over the second coordinate (e.g., “all points with second value ≥ y”). This avoids per-query scanning and enables efficient max aggregation with segment trees or BITs.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts share a consistent offline pattern: sort points by the first constraint (so they can be incrementally added as the threshold changes) and answer each query by querying a data structure keyed by the second constraint using a “max over all qualifying points.” Failures largely come from subtle mapping/indexing mistakes when converting “nums2 ≥ y” (a suffix) into prefix queries for BIT/segment trees, or from using inconsistent coordinate-compression sets (leading to updates/queries that don’t align). Another distinguishing factor is correct sentinel handling: distinguishing “no candidate” from a legitimate maximum (e.g., using sufficiently negative infinity rather than zero), plus careful half-open vs inclusive range semantics. Attempts that nearly work but fail typically do the right high-level algorithm yet botch one of these implementation-critical details.

items.title: Coordinate-Compress the Query Axis
items.description: Coordinate-compress the variable used in your range structure to guarantee consistent updates and queries.
items.content: When the query condition is based on a value that can be large (but only takes values from the data), compress that axis using all candidate values. Then map both updates (point values) and queries (threshold lower_bound results) through the same compression. This prevents mismatches where an update lands in one index scheme but the query asks in another.

items.title: Be Consistent About Range Semantics
items.description: Use a clear convention (inclusive vs half-open) for segment tree queries and stick to it end-to-end.
items.content: Segment trees often differ on whether they query [l, r] or [l, r). Pick one convention, implement it once, and ensure your lower_bound results and the maximum-range endpoints match it. Inconsistent semantics can silently drop valid candidates or include invalid ones, producing wrong maxima only in edge cases.

items.title: Use Correct “No Answer” Sentinels
items.description: Represent “no feasible point” with a robust sentinel that cannot be confused with real maxima.
items.content: For max queries, use a sufficiently small negative infinity sentinel rather than 0 (unless you can prove all valid sums are strictly positive). After querying, translate the sentinel back to -1 (or the required no-solution value) only when no qualifying updates were made. This prevents false positives where the structure’s default looks like a valid maximum.

items.description: Maintain a pointer into the sorted points list so each point is inserted at most once.

items.title: Enforce “count once” with canonical pairing
items.description: When pairs are symmetric, count each valid pair using a canonical rule so it can’t be discovered twice.
items.content: If objects pair by a reversible relation, define a deterministic way to select exactly one direction (e.g., based on ordering or a canonical representative). Then iterate through all objects and count only when the current object is the chosen representative, ensuring each pair contributes at most once.
items.reasoning: The successful approaches rely on a counting strategy that avoids double-counting and correctly enforces “each string used at most once.” They either (1) count only one representative per reversible pair using an ordering rule, or (2) explicitly track consumption via a “used” set or by removing elements from the remaining pool. The partially correct attempts that “almost” work fail when they don’t fully prevent double counting across the two directed scans of a reversible relationship, or when they rely on insufficiently strict consumption logic. The incorrect attempts show common pitfalls: removing from the pool without guarding against counting the same pair twice (due to encountering both members later), and using a too-permissive counting condition that doesn’t guarantee a pair is counted once.

items.title: Use state to prevent reuse (consumption or used-tracking)
items.description: To satisfy “each item used at most once,” maintain explicit “available/used” state and update it when forming a pair.
items.content: Greedy pairing is safe when you consistently “consume” both elements of a chosen pair so they cannot be reused later. Implement this either by removing from a mutable pool (set/count map) or by maintaining a separate used set, and always check membership/availability before pairing.

items.title: Choose the right state: maintain sorted distinct values
items.description: Represent each subarray by the sorted set of its distinct elements and compute the objective from that representation.
items.content: Many order-gap problems depend only on distinct values and the gaps between consecutive distinct values in sorted order, not on multiplicities. Maintain the sorted distinct set as your state, and define the metric in terms of neighbor gaps so updates become well-defined and local.
items.reasoning: The successful attempt uses an incremental data-structure approach on the *right representation*: for each subarray start, it maintains the sorted set of **distinct** values and updates the imbalance using only **local changes** (the predecessor/successor neighbors of an inserted distinct value). Attempts that instead tried to derive a global formula (partially correct attempt 3/4/8/9) either failed to maintain correct conditions for “consecutive in sorted order” (a subtle adjacency notion that is not determined just by value-pair presence), or produced complexity blow-ups by using operations that are effectively O(n) per insertion into a sorted list, leading to O(n^3). Another failure pattern is “overcompressing” the problem into bitset/popcount relations without verifying that the derived relation matches the definition of gaps between **consecutive distinct** sorted values. The key difference is that the correct solution always ties updates to the exact mathematical definition and verifies only the neighborhood affected by each insertion, guaranteeing correctness and manageable complexity.

items.title: Use a Boundary-Invariant State (last-k / run-length DP)
items.description: Track only the minimal suffix information needed to decide future legality.
items.content: When appending fixed-length chunks, any newly formed forbidden pattern must cross the join, so you only need a small boundary state (e.g., last 2 characters or capped run length). Build DP transitions using that reduced state rather than the whole constructed string, which keeps correctness and efficiency.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts generally converged on the idea that the validity of future concatenations depends only on a very small “boundary” context (at most the last 2 characters, or equivalently a capped run length), enabling a compact DP or transition-check. items.title: Constraint-First State Design (Boundary Characters)
items.description: When an operation’s future impact depends only on local interfaces, encode just the interface in the DP state.
items.content: Here, overlap can only occur at the join boundary and depends solely on the current string’s first/last characters. Designing the DP state as (first_char, last_char) makes each transition’s cost computable and ensures optimal substructure.
items.reasoning: Successful attempts converged on the same correct abstraction: the effect of each join on future possibilities depends only on the boundary characters (first and last) of the currently built string, so a DP over (first_char, last_char) is sufficient. items.title: Constraint-Driven DP State + Sentinel
items.description: Use a DP whose recurrence directly encodes the legal-move constraint, and represent unreachable states with a clearly dominated sentinel plus a strict final check.
items.content: When the problem is naturally “best path with constraints,” define dp[state] as the best achievable objective reaching that state, and update it only from predecessors that satisfy the constraint. Use a negative infinity sentinel so that invalid/unreachable predecessors can’t accidentally improve results, and verify reachability at the end.
items.reasoning: All “correct” attempts share the same core modeling: a DP over indices where the transition only depends on which earlier indices can legally jump to the current one, and unreachable states are tracked with a strong negative sentinel plus a final reachability check. Failures/partial correctness come from introducing unnecessary complexity or special-casing without correctly preserving the DP semantics, most notably when trying to optimize via value-based data structures: these approaches must ensure the mapping from “value constraints” to “max over valid predecessors” is correct, and that coordinate compression + range queries don’t accidentally include invalid predecessors or mishandle update/query order. Attempts that simply tried to reason about tricky parameters (like target = 0) or left the logic incomplete also fail because they break the invariant that every dp state must be derived consistently from valid previous states.

items.title: Index-Order Preserves Valid Transitions
items.description: If moves are restricted to increasing indices, iterate in index order so each dp transition only uses already-finalized predecessor states.
items.content: For DAG-like movement rules (e.g., j>i), a simple forward iteration ensures correctness without needing backtracking. This prevents subtle bugs where an optimization might query values that belong to future states.

items.title: Event-Driven Simulation Using the Rule’s Natural Unit
items.description: Simulate in the same discrete “trigger events” that the problem statement uses to define when state changes occur.
items.content: Because the transfer happens immediately after each fixed amount of consumption, using that consumption quantum as your simulation step keeps timing correct and avoids subtle off-by-one or “when does the trigger fire” errors. In similar problems, pick the smallest unit that directly corresponds to a stated rule trigger, then update distance and fuel consistently per step.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts share a clean modeling choice: they simulate the process in the natural “event units” implied by the rule (consuming from the main tank in chunks of five liters) and update state only in ways that respect the instantaneous transfer timing. Several failures stem from incorrect or incomplete accounting—either trying to batch without correctly handling when the “after every 5 liters consumed” event occurs relative to stopping conditions, or attempting to compute the number of injections/total liters without a reliable invariant, leading to mismatch in partial final segments. Partially correct attempts show the hazard of partial shortcuts: they start counting but break the simulation logic when the last incomplete batch matters or when the state transition (main reaching empty) interacts with the injection rule.

items.title: Build and extend a maximal valid run
items.description: When a subarray must satisfy local adjacency rules, compute maximal contiguous segments that already satisfy those rules, then take the best length over valid starts.
items.content: Reformulate the constraint into a property of consecutive elements (e.g., all values are within a bound and adjacent parity alternates). For each possible starting position that satisfies the required start condition, extend greedily until the first violation, because any shorter subarray within that maximal run can’t beat the maximal length.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts share a simple, robust structure: they scan for indices that can start a valid subarray, then greedily extend to the maximal contiguous alternating-parity stretch under the threshold, updating the best length. The failures (partially correct and the more “risky” full attempts) typically come from incorrect handling of what to do after a violation—most notably, updating left boundaries using local parity comparisons without a guaranteed invariant that the candidate window remains valid, or making jumps that might skip potential valid starts. Another common issue is trying to infer the next valid start from parity logic without actually tracking the boundary conditions that define validity (start must be even and <= threshold; every adjacent pair must alternate parity). The best solutions avoid these pitfalls by either keeping the window definition implicit as “maximal valid run starting at current start” or by using a two-phase scan (choose a start, extend while constraints hold) without relying on fragile “adjustments” to the left index.

items.title: Difference-Constraint Reformulation

items.content: For alternating/subsequence-pattern problems, rewrite the condition in terms of what must happen between neighbors (e.g., consecutive differences) because this yields simple local checks. Then the global pattern can be verified by ensuring each new step matches the required next-difference sign.

items.title: Constraint-Driven Greedy with Forced Decisions
items.description: Make the greedy choice that forces the current position to exactly satisfy the target, using prior decisions to determine the remaining requirement.
items.content: When operations affect contiguous ranges in one direction, iterate left-to-right and compute the already-imposed cumulative effect at the current index. Then choose the unique amount of operations starting here that makes the current value meet the final constraint (e.g., reduce it to exactly zero); if that amount would violate feasibility, fail immediately. This avoids branching because any deviation from the forced amount cannot be repaired later under the range structure.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts share a greedy “left-to-right” structure backed by a range-difference representation: at each index, they compute the current cumulative effect from previously chosen operations, then force the current element to become zero by choosing exactly the necessary number of operations starting at that index. The failures/partial solutions typically arise when they mishandle the update directions/signs (confusing add vs subtract in the difference array), or when they compute the required amount using an inconsistent definition of “current effect” (e.g., mixing whether the running value is already applied to nums or is the negative decrement), leading to off-by-sign errors that only show up on later indices. Another distinguishing factor is boundary handling: correct versions explicitly prevent starting operations too late, and verify that the tail cannot be fixed later, while partially correct versions sometimes assume nonnegativity implicitly without enforcing the tail condition strongly enough or schedule effects with the wrong diff endpoint.

items.title: Use a Difference Array to Track Range Effects
items.description: Represent repeated range updates efficiently by tracking only where the effect starts and ends, not every element.
items.content: For range-decrement/increment operations, maintain a difference array and a running accumulator to know the net effect at each index. Each time you “schedule” t operations on a length-k window, update the difference at the window start and at the window end boundary, then let the running sum propagate this effect naturally. This both improves performance and reduces bookkeeping errors compared to directly simulating each operation.

items.title: Constraint-to-Knapsack Modeling
items.description: Rewrite the combinatorics (unique integers, order doesn’t matter) as a subset-selection problem and solve it with 0/1 knapsack DP.
items.content: When elements must be unique and only their sum matters, represent each allowable value as an item and count subsets whose weights sum to the target. A DP where each item is considered once naturally enforces uniqueness and handles order-independence without extra bookkeeping.
items.reasoning: All successful attempts share the same core modeling: map the “sum of unique x-th powers of positive integers” to counting subsets where each power can be used at most once, then solve via 0/1 knapsack (subset-sum counting) DP. They also correctly handle orderlessness by iterating over powers as items and updating DP in a way that prevents reuse. The incorrect attempt fails by applying an unnecessary “subtract the empty subset” correction; while it uses the right DP recurrence, it introduces an unjustified post-processing step that can break correctness even when intuition suggests it “shouldn’t matter.”

items.title: Use Item-Processing Order to Enforce Uniqueness
items.description: In 0/1 subset-sum counting, iterate the target sum in descending order for each item to prevent reusing the same item multiple times.
items.content: A standard invariant for 0/1 knapsack DP is that after processing a given item, dp[s] depends only on states from before that item (not multiple usages). Descending iteration over the sum dimension ensures transitions dp[s] ← dp[s] + dp[s−w] don’t chain within the same item.

items.title: Avoid Unwarranted Corrections After DP
items.description: Don’t “patch” counts with ad-hoc adjustments unless you can explicitly prove they change exactly the intended cases.
items.content: The incorrect solution modified the DP result by subtracting a term meant to remove the empty subset. Even if the adjustment seems harmless, post-processing is risky—always verify which configurations the DP actually counts for the specific target state before applying subtract/add corrections.

items.title: Enforce a Window Invariant with Precise Boundary Updates
items.description: Maintain an explicit invariant (no forbidden substring inside the current window) and update the window boundary based on exact forbidden matches that violate it.
items.content: When scanning left-to-right, keep a left boundary such that the current window is valid. If you find a forbidden substring occurrence that ends at the current position, move the left boundary to one past that occurrence’s start (or past the maximum required among them), ensuring the invariant is restored immediately.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts share a consistent correct invariant: maintain a sliding window (or equivalent boundary/D P) such that the current substring contains no forbidden pattern, and when a forbidden match is detected that ends at the current position, the left boundary is moved just past the earliest starting position that would still keep validity. They exploit the key constraint that forbidden lengths are bounded (by a small maximum), allowing checking only a limited number of candidate substrings ending at each index. The partially correct attempts either build similar structures but use inconsistent data semantics (e.g., “bad end” as a boolean rather than capturing the specific start positions needed to update the boundary correctly) or use sets/lists incorrectly for membership checks, or apply a DP/inequality without fully validating the mapping between “suffix length” and forbidden match constraints. Overall, correctness depends on (1) representing enough information to update the boundary precisely, and (2) applying the update rule in a way that is guaranteed to preserve the “no forbidden substring” invariant.

items.title: Use Bounded-Length Constraints to Limit Candidate Checks
items.description: Convert a global constraint into a local search rule by leveraging a small maximum forbidden length so you only check O(maxLen) candidates per position.
items.content: If each forbidden pattern has length at most K (small), then any newly created violation when extending to position r must be a substring ending at r with length ≤ K. This lets you group forbidden patterns by length and check only those short suffixes, making sliding-window or DP approaches efficient and verifiable.

items.title: Track Logical Orientation Instead of Reversing Strings
items.description: Represent the state of the buffer using a boolean “orientation” and update how new characters are placed without ever performing full reversals.
items.content: When operations reverse the entire current content, you can avoid costly or error-prone physical reversals by maintaining a direction/orientation flag. Use that flag to decide whether each incoming non-trigger character should be appended to a “front” stream or a “back” stream in a way consistent with the current logical orientation.
items.reasoning: Successful attempts consistently model the effect of each reversal as a “logical orientation” (a flag) and maintain two buckets/streams for non-reversal characters depending on whether they are typed while the buffer is logically normal or reversed. They then reconstruct the final output with the correct combination order (typically one side appended in reverse entry order). Incorrect attempts often tried to infer the final string from a remaining contiguous substring or from pointer updates alone; this fails because reversals change relative order, not just which characters remain. Another failure mode was assuming the final orientation determines whether to reverse at the end when, in fact, the extraction order is more subtle and must be tracked during processing.

items.title: Use Two-Stream Reconstruction for Reversal-Flip Problems
items.description: If reversals swap the relative order of everything typed so far, store characters in two sequences corresponding to when they were typed under each orientation, then stitch them in the correct order.
items.content: Split collected characters into two lists/streams: those typed while orientation is normal and those typed while it is reversed. After processing all input, reconstruct the final string by concatenating the appropriate stream(s), reversing exactly one side as needed to match the order induced by the flips.

items.title: Don’t Reduce Order-Complex Operations to a Remaining Substring
items.description: When flips affect relative ordering globally, pointer arithmetic that selects a surviving contiguous segment is usually insufficient.
items.content: Reversing the whole buffer does more than “delete” characters; it permutes their order. Therefore, computing the result as a slice of the original string (plus an optional final reverse) often fails because the kept characters’ relative order depends on intermediate reversal times.

items.description: Be cautious with “reverse at the end” logic: final orientation alone may not determine where each character lands.

Confirm reconstruction rules (which side comes first and whether one side must be reversed) by reasoning with a small trace, rather than relying solely on the final flag.

items.title: Constraint-First Prefix Reformulation
items.description: Re-express subarray properties as a modular equation on prefix sums, then count matching prefix residues.
items.content: First translate the subarray condition into an equation involving a prefix-sum difference (e.g., count in subarray = pref[r+1] − pref[l]). Then reduce that equation modulo m to get a direct relation between pref[l] mod m and pref[r+1] mod m. Finally, count subarrays by summing frequency matches of these required residue classes.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts share a common robust pattern: convert the original condition into a binary/counting feature, then use prefix sums and modular arithmetic to translate the subarray condition into a constraint on pairs of prefix residues. The failing/partially-correct attempt typically makes an incorrect algebraic sign/offset when deriving the “needed” residue for matching prefix states (e.g., using “+k” where “-k” is required). Another recurring risk is mixing up whether the “current” prefix residue should be updated before querying the frequency map, which can shift the subarray endpoints. Overall, success correlates with (1) precise derivation of the modular equation and (2) consistent ordering of update/query relative to the prefix definition.

items.title: Validate Modular Offset Algebra
items.description: Carefully re-derive the modular “need” term to avoid sign/offset mistakes that silently break counting.
items.content: When you manipulate (pref[r+1] − pref[l]) % m == k, explicitly move terms and keep track of whether the target residue involves “pref[r+1] − k” or “pref[r+1] + k” (sign errors are common). A quick consistency check—plugging a minimal example subarray or verifying units/directions of the offset—can catch incorrect “need” formulas before implementation.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts share a common robust pattern: convert the original condition into a binary/counting feature, then use prefix sums and modular arithmetic to translate the subarray condition into a constraint on pairs of prefix residues. The failing/partially-correct attempt typically makes an incorrect algebraic sign/offset when deriving the “needed” residue for matching prefix states (e.g., using “+k” where “-k” is required). Another recurring risk is mixing up whether the “current” prefix residue should be updated before querying the frequency map, which can shift the subarray endpoints. Overall, success correlates with (1) precise derivation of the modular equation and (2) consistent ordering of update/query relative to the prefix definition.

items.title: Update-Then-Query vs Query-Then-Update Discipline
items.description: Maintain strict consistency between your prefix definition and when you update the prefix state during iteration.
items.content: If prefix sums represent counts before processing the current element, update the prefix residue after adding the current element’s contribution, and query frequencies using the updated residue (or vice versa) consistently with that definition. In prefix-residue counting, an off-by-one in update order changes which prefix endpoints correspond to subarrays and leads to incorrect totals.

items.title: Constraint-First Recursive DP
items.description: Model the operation as a recursive feasibility check where every immediate result of a split must satisfy the rule.
items.content: When an operation imposes conditions at each intermediate step, reduce the problem to checking whether a segment can be fully reduced via a recursion/DP that validates the constraints for each child segment at the moment it is created. This prevents “end-state only” reasoning that ignores intermediate legality.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts share a common meta-pattern: they formalize the splitting rule as a recursive/DP validity condition on subarrays, and they explicitly distinguish “immediate child” constraints (a side produced by a split must itself satisfy length==1 or sum>=m) from what could be achieved after further splits. Unsuccessful attempts either over-assumed simplifications (e.g., “total sum >= m implies always possible”) or replaced the true recursive constraint with a too-weak surrogate (e.g., only requiring that some partition blocks have sum>=m), which breaks because the rule is enforced at every intermediate split, not just at the end. Another failure pattern is using greedy intuitions about positive numbers/contiguity without proving that the intermediate-step constraints are always satisfiable; attempts that omitted interval recursion/verification tend to produce false criteria.

items.title: Validate “Immediate Child” vs “Eventually” Requirements
items.description: Do not allow a transformation to postpone satisfaction of a constraint to later splits unless the rule explicitly permits it.
items.content: A common bug is assuming that if a larger segment has sufficient sum, then after further splitting its parts will automatically be valid; but if the rule checks each subarray at creation time, then each produced part must already satisfy the condition. Add an explicit verification layer in the recurrence to enforce these “local” constraints.

items.title: Avoid Over-Simplified Global Criteria
items.description: Only rely on global measures (like total sum) after proving they are sufficient for all intermediate steps.
items.content: Global conditions often look promising with positive numbers/monotonicity, but they may be insufficient because legality depends on the structure of intermediate subarrays. items.title: Use Interval DP When Splits Are Structural
items.description: For contiguous-array splitting problems, represent states by subarray intervals rather than by counts or prefix sums alone.
items.content: When operations split an interval into two contiguous parts, interval DP (with transitions over split points) naturally captures contiguity and hierarchical constraints. This usually yields correctness at acceptable cost when n is small enough.

items.title: Constraint-to-Capacity Reformulation
items.description: Reformulate combinatorial construction constraints into an aggregate “capacity vs. required total” inequality parameterized by the decision variable.
items.content: When groups must satisfy structural rules (like increasing lengths) and elements have per-item usage limits with “at most once per group” distinctness, model each element as contributing to at most k groups. Then reduce feasibility for building k groups to comparing total available contributions against the exact required total placements implied by the group-size pattern.
items.reasoning: Most “correct” attempts share a common high-level pattern: reformulate the group construction into a capacity-feasibility test that depends only on the candidate number of groups k, and then use binary search over k. The key modeling step is treating each element’s limit as a maximum number of groups it can participate in (at most once per group), yielding a clean aggregate condition of the form sum(min(limit_i, k)) ≥ k(k+1)/2. Attempts that were only partially correct typically either (a) got the reduction almost right but left unjustified assumptions about sufficiency, (b) introduced unnecessary heap/greedy assignment that risked incorrect distribution claims, or (c) used inconsistent feasibility logic (e.g., incorrect bounds, overcomplicated invariants, or “slots” interpretations not properly grounded in the within-group distinctness constraint).

items.title: Binary Search on Monotone Feasibility
items.description: If you can test whether a given k is feasible and feasibility is monotone in k, maximize k via binary search.
items.content: Many such problems naturally have a monotonic property: if you can build k groups, you can also build any smaller number. Use binary search over k, with a fast feasibility check derived from the reformulation, to avoid unreliable direct greedy construction.

items.title: Justify “Sufficiency,” Not Only Necessity
items.description: Be cautious with feasibility conditions that are only necessary; ensure the model also captures why satisfying the inequality guarantees an actual construction.
items.content: Some attempts used a correct-looking inequality but failed to argue that it is sufficient under the original constraints (especially within-group distinctness and cross-group reuse). A successful approach either relies on a known proven reduction or includes a reasoning why the aggregate capacity condition fully characterizes feasibility for the specific structure of group sizes.

items.title: Avoid Overcomplicated Assignment Greedies
items.description: Prefer invariant/capacity checks over step-by-step greedy allocations when the latter is hard to prove.
items.content: Heap-based or “assign to largest unmet groups” strategies are easy to implement but often fail because the distribution argument is subtle. If the problem admits a clean reduction to a single inequality, use that—then binary search—rather than trusting an intuitive but hard-to-verify greedy assignment.

items.title: Bound Search Range Safely
items.description: When binary searching, choose bounds that are guaranteed not to exclude the optimum, even if they are not tight.
items.content: Correct solutions cap k by a value that must hold for any feasible construction (e.g., number of groups cannot exceed the number of distinct values). Partially correct attempts sometimes used loose or inconsistent upper bounds or assumptions, which can lead to incorrect logic or wasted computation.
